summary: German Idealist philosopher who wrote The Phenomenology of Spirit and Elements of the Philosophy of Right. Known for the dialectical method (thesis-antithesis-synthesis/Aufhebung), the master-slave dialectic, the concept of Geist (spirit), absolute idealism, and his influential lectures on the philosophy of history. His Young/Left Hegelian followers included Marx, Feuerbach, and Stirner.

works.name: The Phenomenology of Spirit and the Dialectic
works.description: The Phenomenology of Spirit (1807) is Hegel's most famous work, in which consciousness moves through progressively higher levels before arriving at 'absolute knowledge.' It introduces the concept of Geist (spirit), which discovers itself through self-alienation. The 'master-slave dialectic' (lord-bondsman) describes two self-conscious beings recognizing each other through a 'struggle to the death,' where the bondsman experiences 'absolute negativity' via fear before rediscovering himself through labor. The book uses the word aufheben for 'sublation,' whereby contradictions of Being and Nothing are resolved in Becoming. Alexandre Kojeve's influential reading argues that the USA and USSR represented two interpretations of Hegel.

clues.clue: Master-slave dialectic (lord-bondsman): struggle to the death for recognition; bondsman rediscovers self through labor
examples: describes how two figures recognize each other through a 'struggle to the death'
Alexandre Kojeve argued that a figure theorized by this thinker is the key to historical progress

clues.clue: Consciousness moves through progressively higher levels to 'absolute knowledge'
examples: wrote a work whose narrative moves through progressively higher levels of consciousness before finally arriving at 'absolute knowledge'

clues.clue: Aufhebung (sublation): contradictions of Being and Nothing resolved in Becoming
examples: theorized a process of sublation through which the contradictions of 'Being' and 'Nothing' were resolved in their transformation into 'Becoming'
uses the word aufheben to denote the 'sublation' of concepts by their opposites

clues.clue: Dialectic often summarized as thesis-antithesis-synthesis (actually from Chalybaus/Fichte)
examples: often summarized with the formula 'thesis-antithesis-synthesis'
A model erroneously attributed to this thinker is actually an interpretation by Heinrich Moritz Chalybaus

examples: placed this philosopher's name inside quotation marks when analyzing his claim that the 'monotony' of the Bhagavad Gita reflects the static nature of India

clues.clue: Jean Hippolyte called it a 'philosophical novel'
examples: Jean Hippolyte, a French translator of this 1807 book, called it a 'philosophical novel'

works.name: Elements of the Philosophy of Right and Ethical Life
works.description: In the Philosophy of Right, Hegel develops the concept of Sittlichkeit (ethical life), which encompasses family, civil society, and the state. Abstract right, morality, and ethical life form three spheres. Property is 'embodiment of personality' and an 'abstract right' through 'mutual recognition.' The state is 'the march of God in the world' and 'no ideal work of art.' The preface contains the famous 'owl of Minerva' metaphor: philosophy always arrives too late, like the owl flying at dusk. He rejected democratic electoral systems in favor of representation by economic 'corporations.'

clues.clue: Three spheres: abstract right, morality, and Sittlichkeit (ethical life: family, civil society, state)
examples: considered conduct vis-a-vis family, society, and the state to belong to the sphere of 'ethical life'
presented the state, civil society, and family as respectively representing moments of individuality, particularity, and universality

clues.clue: Owl of Minerva: philosophy arrives too late, 'spreads its wings only with the falling of the dusk'
examples: philosophy is always too late to make a difference, using the example of the Owl of Minerva only flying at dusk
the 'owl of Minerva spreads its wings only with the falling of the dusk'

clues.clue: The state is 'the march of God in the world' and 'no ideal work of art'
examples: called the state 'is no ideal work of art'
he claimed that, like 'a criminal,' the state is 'always a living man' in a section calling it 'the march of God in the world'

clues.clue: Property is 'embodiment of personality' and 'abstract right' through 'mutual recognition'
examples: property can also be considered an 'embodiment of personality'
private property defines individuality, it is not a natural right, but rather a socially constructed 'mutual recognition'

clues.clue: Rejected democratic elections; favored representation by economic 'corporations'
examples: rejected democratic electoral systems on the grounds that they divide the populace by geography, instead of by economic sectors

clues.clue: Robert Brandom's A Spirit of Trust interprets Phenomenology to examine 'transcendental conditions of determinately contentful conceptual norms'
examples: this 2019 book, the author tries to interpret the Phenomenology

works.name: Geist (Spirit) and History
works.description: Geist (spirit) is central to Hegel's philosophy. The Absolute Spirit reconciles Subjective and Objective parts through art, religion, and philosophy. History is the process whereby spirit discovers itself and realizes freedom through 'world-historical figures' like Napoleon (the Weltgeist, world-spirit). The Lectures on the Philosophy of History divide history into original, reflective, and philosophical approaches, introduce the 'cunning of reason' and the term 'Volksgeist,' and infamously claim Africa 'has no history.' Freedom progresses: 'one man is free' -> 'some men are free' -> 'all men are free.' Kojeve's reading of Hegel's theory of history influenced Fukuyama's End of History.

clues.clue: Geist (spirit) drives history; Absolute Spirit reconciles Subjective and Objective through art, religion, philosophy
examples: Art, religion, and philosophy arise from the Absolute version of this concept
described both using his concept of dialectic

clues.clue: Lectures on the Philosophy of History: history = spirit discovering freedom; 'world-historical individuals' like Napoleon
examples: this thing realizes its freedom through self-recognition
called them 'world-historical individuals'

clues.clue: Africa 'has no history' / 'enveloped in the dark mantle of night'
examples: infamously claims that Africa is not part of it
dismisses Africa as 'enveloped in the dark mantle of night'

clues.clue: 'Cunning of reason': world-historical individuals unconsciously carry out purposes of the World Spirit
examples: coins this three-word phrase to describe how world-historical individuals unconsciously carry out the purposes of the World Spirit

clues.clue: History divided into original, reflective, and philosophical approaches
examples: divided history into original, reflective, and philosophical approaches

clues.clue: Freedom progresses: Orientals (one free), Greeks (some free), modern men (all free)
examples: contrasted Orientals, Greeks, and modern men based on their views of freedom

clues.clue: Introduced the term 'Volksgeist'
examples: Hegel's lectures on this concept introduced the term 'Volksgeist'

works.name: Science of Logic
works.description: The Science of Logic (1812) has sections on Being, Essence, and Concept. It calls logic a 'realm of shadows,' rejects the law of non-contradiction, and arrives at 'pure thought,' which Hegel compares to God 'in his essence before the creation of nature.' In the Shorter Logic, Hegel describes the unity of the 'absolute idea' using Aristotle's phrase 'thought thinking itself' (noesis noeseos noesis). J.M.E. McTaggart wrote several commentaries.

clues.clue: Sections on Being, Essence, and Concept; calls logic a 'realm of shadows'; rejects law of non-contradiction
examples: divides the title subject into being, essence, and concept
calls its title discipline a 'realm of shadows' and rejects the law of non-contradiction

clues.clue: Arrives at 'pure thought' = God 'in his essence before the creation of nature'
examples: arrives at 'pure thought,' which he modestly compares to God 'in his essence before the creation of nature'

clues.clue: Shorter Logic: 'thought thinking itself' (noesis noeseos noesis) describes the 'absolute idea'
examples: uses this phrase to describe the unity of the 'absolute idea'

works.name: Aesthetics
works.description: Hegel's Lectures on Aesthetics distinguish between classical, romantic, and symbolic art. He argued classical art was the home of 'ideal beauty' and that art 'in its highest vocation remains for us a thing of the past' (the 'end of art' thesis, modernized by Arthur Danto with Warhol's Brillo Boxes). Poetry is the most 'inward' and perfect form of art. His reading of Antigone as illustrating the 'supreme opposition in ethics' (man vs. woman) is critiqued by Kierkegaard in Either/Or.

clues.clue: Aesthetics: classical (ideal beauty), romantic, and symbolic art; art 'remains for us a thing of the past'
examples: distinguished between classical, romantic, and symbolic art
art 'in its highest vocation...remains for us a thing of the past'

clues.clue: Arthur Danto modernized end-of-art thesis via Warhol's Brillo Boxes
examples: Arthur Danto's essay on 'The End of Art'
Arthur Danto presented a modern version of this man's thesis

clues.clue: Poetry is the most 'inward' and perfect form of art
examples: Hegel called this medium the most 'inward,' and thus most perfect, form of art

clues.clue: Antigone illustrates 'supreme opposition in ethics' between man and woman (Sittlichkeit section)
examples: claims illustrates the 'supreme opposition in ethics' individualized in 'the opposing natures of man and woman'

works.name: Young Hegelians and Legacy
works.description: After Hegel's death in 1831, his followers split into Right (Old) Hegelians aligned with the Lutheran church and Left (Young) Hegelians who rejected religious orthodoxy. The Young Hegelians met at Hippel's Weinstube in 1840s Berlin and included Feuerbach, Marx, Stirner, Bruno Bauer, David Friedrich Strauss (who coined the terms), and Moses Hess. Kierkegaard attacked Hegelianism as 'the system,' replacing 'the truth is the whole' with truth as subjectivity. British Idealists (F.H. Bradley, T.H. Green, Bosanquet, McTaggart) were influenced by Hegel. The Pittsburgh school (McDowell, Brandom) revives Hegel. Slavoj Zizek identifies as a Hegelian.

clues.clue: Young/Left Hegelians: Feuerbach, Marx, Stirner, Bruno Bauer; met at Hippel's Weinstube; split after 1831
examples: this movement split from its right-leaning counterpart and flourished at the University of Berlin after its namesake philosopher died in 1831
Max Stirner and Karl Marx were among the 'left' followers

clues.clue: David Friedrich Strauss coined Left/Right Hegelian terms while defending The Life of Jesus
examples: The terms Left Hegelian and Right Hegelian were coined by this thinker while defending his book The Life of Jesus

clues.clue: Kierkegaard attacked Hegelianism as 'the system'; replaced 'truth is the whole' with truth as subjectivity
examples: 'the truth is the whole' is replaced with truth as subjectivity in Concluding Unscientific Postscript by Kierkegaard

clues.clue: British Idealists: F.H. Bradley (Appearance and Reality, lump of sugar regress), T.H. Green, Bosanquet, McTaggart
examples: The British idealists were influenced by this philosopher's introduction of the concept of 'the Absolute'

clues.clue: G.E. Moore and Bertrand Russell revolted against British Idealism

clues.clue: Pittsburgh school: McDowell (Mind and World), Brandom (Making It Explicit) revive Hegel

clues.clue: Slavoj Zizek identifies more as Hegelian than Marxist

examples: This man was the chief influence on Marx's theory of historical materialism

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Hegel is identified as a German Idealist philosopher, author of The Phenomenology of Spirit, popularizer of absolute idealism. He used the example of water boiling to illustrate how change in quality doesn't necessarily produce change in quantity ('measure'). He called philosophical work a 'Penelope's web' in the Philosophy of Right. His concept of 'the Absolute' was central. Jakob Bohme was called 'the first German philosopher' by Hegel. Hegel was Schelling's roommate and later rival. The Kyoto School's dialectic differs from Hegel's by returning things to a pre-opposition state.

clues.clue: German Idealist; author of Phenomenology of Spirit; popularized absolute idealism
examples: this German idealist who wrote The Phenomenology of Spirit
Hegel popularized an 'absolute' version of this view

clues.clue: Water boiling: change in quality doesn't necessarily produce change in quantity ('measure')
examples: used the example of water boiling to illustrate how a change in quality does not necessarily result in a change in quantity

clues.clue: Called Jakob Bohme 'the first German philosopher'
examples: called Bohme the 'first German philosopher'

clues.clue: Gadamer's five hermeneutical studies titled after Hegel's 'inverted world'
examples: discussion of the 'inverted world' was analyzed in a series of five hermeneutical studies by Gadamer

clues.clue: Gillian Rose's Hegel Contra Sociology
examples: A book that argues that this philosopher preemptively criticized the traditions of Durkheim and Weber

clues.clue: Kyoto School: Nishida's dialectic returns to pre-opposition state, unlike Hegel's sublation
examples: Nishida frequently clarified that the logic of place's dialectic returns things to a pre-opposition state, unlike this German philosopher's version

comprehensive_summary:
Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel is among the most frequently clued philosophers in quizbowl, with 18 tossups and 47 bonus appearances. He is universally identified as the German Idealist author of The Phenomenology of Spirit.

The Phenomenology of Spirit (1807) traces consciousness through progressively higher levels to 'absolute knowledge.' Its central passage is the master-slave dialectic (lord-bondsman), in which two self-conscious beings recognize each other through a 'struggle to the death'; the bondsman experiences 'absolute negativity' via fear before rediscovering himself through labor. Susan Buck-Morss argues this passage was influenced by the Haitian Revolution. The concept of Aufhebung (sublation) resolves contradictions: Being and Nothing synthesize into Becoming. The thesis-antithesis-synthesis model is often attributed to Hegel but actually comes from Heinrich Moritz Chalybaus/Fichte. Alexandre Kojeve's influential reading shaped Fukuyama's End of History.

The Philosophy of Right develops Sittlichkeit (ethical life) through three spheres: abstract right, morality, and ethical life (family, civil society, state). Its preface contains the owl of Minerva metaphor: philosophy arrives too late, like the owl flying at dusk. The state is 'the march of God in the world' and 'no ideal work of art.'

Geist (spirit) is central: the Absolute Spirit reconciles Subjective and Objective through art, religion, and philosophy. The Lectures on the Philosophy of History describe history as spirit discovering freedom through 'world-historical figures' like Napoleon (Weltgeist). They introduce the 'cunning of reason,' the term 'Volksgeist,' and infamously claim Africa 'has no history.'

The Science of Logic has sections on Being, Essence, and Concept, rejects the law of non-contradiction, and in the Shorter Logic uses Aristotle's 'thought thinking itself' for the absolute idea.

Hegel's Lectures on Aesthetics distinguish classical, romantic, and symbolic art; argue art 'remains for us a thing of the past' (Danto modernized this via Warhol); and call poetry the most perfect art form. His reading of Antigone was critiqued by Kierkegaard.

After his death in 1831, followers split into Right/Old Hegelians (Lutheran church) and Left/Young Hegelians (Feuerbach, Marx, Stirner, Bruno Bauer, D.F. Strauss). Kierkegaard attacked 'the system.' British Idealists (Bradley, Green, McTaggart) extended his work until Moore and Russell's revolt. The Pittsburgh school (McDowell, Brandom) revives Hegel today. Zizek identifies as Hegelian.
